title: ECE Comprehensive Program Review   2023-2024 (Part 1)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Comprehensive Program Review 2023-2024
Submitted: 12/16/2024
• Early Childhood Education and Child Development (ECE)
• Patrice Thatcher-Stephens, Program Coordinator
1. Description of Program:
a) Organization Staffing and Structure:
The ECE—Child Development, Family and Education Program is organized as a Career and Technical
Education Program under the purview of the Interim Dean of CTE as follows
• President/Superintendent of College of the Siskiyous
• Vice President of Academic Affairs
• Interim Dean Career and Technical Education
• Program Coordinator and Full Time Tenured Instructor
• Experienced and fully qualified Adjuncts hired on semester by semester basis
b) Primary Purpose:
The ECE Program at College of the Siskiyous serves two equally important purposes. We prepare front-
line early educators for work throughout the geographical and expanded online area served by the college.
Equally importantly, we prepare students for transfer to 4-year institutions through our ADT degrees.
Additionally, our course of study provides the classes required through CDE Title 5 Child Development Permits.
c) Whom we Serve
Geographic Service Area
Siskiyou County, adjacent to Oregon in the northern, inland region of California, is the fifth largest
county in the state. More than sixty percent of Siskiyou County’s land is managed by federal and state
government agencies. These include the USDA Forest Service, the Bureau of Land Management, the U.S. Fish
and Wildlife Service, and the California Department of Fish and Game. The county’s 6,347 square miles
encompass a wide variety of geographic areas and community structures.
Siskiyou County is one of the most sparsely populated counties in California. Our students have
historically been spread throughout an area averaging approximately seven persons per square mile. Our
students are often geographically isolated and served through videoconference sites throughout the county as
well as at the main Campus in Weed and our site in Yreka. Our ECE@COS students are both service providers
and clients of family support services. The primary employers of College of the Siskiyous Early Childhood
Education students are the California State Preschool, as administered through various entities, Head Start
and Early Head Start, as administered through four different entities and a few local independent early
education programs. We also serve family childcare providers. For over 20 years, using videoconference and
online technology, we reached far distant sites in Siskiyou County. Based on research indicating that online
instruction is not Best Practice in training hands-on early educators, we resisted offering Core Classes online in
spite of increasing success rates, excellent online instructors, and cohorts of technically sophisticated students
throughout California consistently requesting that they be able to complete the degree online.
The COVID19 Emergency, significantly impacted our Methods of Instruction, and, ultimately, our
geographic service area beginning in February of Spring Semester 2021. ECE@COS-and virtually all CCC ECE-CD
programs throughout the state- responded immediately and effectively to new circumstances. This necessity,
in effect, created an online program/Zoom MOI serving students throughout the state. These changes are
apparently destined to far outlive the COVID 19 emergency.

title: ECE Comprehensive Program Review   2023-2024 (Part 3)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Comprehensive Program Review 2023-2024
Submitted: 12/16/2024
• Early Childhood Education and Child Development (ECE)
• Patrice Thatcher-Stephens, Program Coordinator
d) Services Provided and e) Delivery Methods
ECE@COS continues to prepare front-line early educators for work throughout the geographical and
expanded online area served by the college. Equally importantly, we prepare students for transfer to 4-year
institutions through our ADT degrees. Additionally, our course of study provides the classes required through
CDE Title 5 Child Development Permits. Based on our 2023 Student Location Matrix and Planning documents,
our services are specifically designed to serve both local and distant students as they prepare to enter the
ever-evolving field of Early Education.
As we prepare these students we are serving families in Siskiyou County and beyond. Through our
Advisory Committee Network, we build upon collaboration with our partners locally and statewide.
Our course of study offers a rigorous and realistic curriculum. Our courses are behind in the process of
Curriculum Committee Program Update. We need to catch up on “routine maintenance”. Unfortunately, we
have been distracted during the past year by challenges in restructuring our lab school collaboration and
becoming reaccredited through the National Association for the Education of Young Children, NAEYC.
Maintaining effective Practicum and Observation sites is dependent on our work with the California Early
Childhood Mentor Program and our collaborating agency.
Following the Two-Year Course Rotation, students continue to be able to complete a local A.A. Degree,
a Transfer Degree to any California State University or articulate, as a junior, to Southern Oregon University’s
online Early Childhood Development Program. In this pattern all ECE@COS classes are offered through
Distance Learning.
Our Rotation of Courses continues to offer classes leading to all levels of California State Child
Development Permits issued through the California State Department Commission on Teacher Credentialing.
In these patterns all ECE@COS courses are offered through Distance Learning.
Our Rotation of Courses continues to offer classes leading to all levels early educator qualifications included in
Title 22 Division 12 from the California State Department of Social Services Community Care Licensing, Teacher
Qualifications. In this pattern all ECE@COS courses are offered through Distance Learning.
Actual Students Served and Services Provided
Our students range in age and demographics from AP high school students or recent high school
graduates, living at home or in the on-campus dorms, to experienced early educators near retirement age who
are challenged to meet the increasing education requirements in our field.
Our students are primarily female, although we are making gradual, but steady, progress enrolling
Perkins Defined Special Populations. A number of males in our ECE and General Education Classes. We
currently have 2 active and committed males in the program. We collaborate effectively with Student Access
Services to serve eligible students with a wide variety of special needs ranging from cerebral palsy to dyslexia
to profound deafness.
Ethnicity and Race
Based on data from the Annie E. Casey Foundation “Kids County Data Center”, reflecting through 2018
and most recent census data, the total population of Siskiyou County The 5 largest ethnic groups in Siskiyou
County, CA are White 83%, Latino 14%, American Indian & Alaska Native 5.2%, Asian 1.7%, Pacific Islander .4%,
Multiracial 5.8%. Interestingly the addition of the Multiracial category is reflected in decreased numbers
identified in all single- race categories. Annie E. Casey Data
The ECE@COS data on race is interesting and moderately encouraging. Our students—who will work
with the children reflected in the Annie E. Casey Foundation “Kids County Data Center 2022”- are far more
diverse than the general adult population in our county.

title: ECE Comprehensive Program Review   2023-2024 (Part 4)
text:
-- 2 of 14 --

College of the Siskiyous Comprehensive Program Review 2023-2024
Submitted: 12/16/2024
• Early Childhood Education and Child Development (ECE)
• Patrice Thatcher-Stephens, Program Coordinator
Program Services and Methods of Delivery
• We have not historically provided Noncredit Courses. Non-credit classes do not meet the academic,
permit and transfer needs, of our students. Combined with the promotion of Dual and Concurrent
Enrollment classes- this was just too many moving parts. However, beginning in 2024 we are in
collaboration with Adult Education and Yreka High School to support their initiative to offer Early
Childhood Education “pre-degree” classes, noncredit, but reflecting the philosophy and intent of
ECE@COS classes.
• We do not provide Basic Skills Courses.
o Basic Skills classes do not meet the academic, permit and transfer needs, of our students.
o We do value COS’s Basic Skills preparation and these courses support out students’ success.
Through the CDTC subsidy program (see below) we award a cash stipend to qualifying students
for Basic Skills units.
• Degree Applicable, Transfer Level and AD-T Classes Face-to-Face/Videoconferenced/Online
o Local A.A. Degrees
o AD-T Degrees
o Child Development Permits at all levels through the Commission on Teacher Credentialing
• Supplemental academic support relating to Counseling/Advising
o Delivered through consistent and collaborative work with COS academic advisors and
counselors. The COVID 19 Emergency prompted our Counseling and Advising Services to
expand access to their services to online students.
o Through our consistent Two-Year Course Rotation and our ECE @COS Program Guide we offer
students a clear road map to meeting their educational and professional goals.
• We provide exemplary support for students with Special Needs and Disabilities.
o Delivered through collaboration with COS’ SAS program.

title: ECE Comprehensive Program Review   2023-2024 (Part 5)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Comprehensive Program Review 2023-2024
Submitted: 12/16/2024
• Early Childhood Education and Child Development (ECE)
• Patrice Thatcher-Stephens, Program Coordinator
• Program Coordinator works with ECE Curriculum Alignment Project Work group and monitors CAP
alignment at College of the Siskiyous. This initiative led directly to our early approval as an ECE AD-T
degree through the CCCCO.
o Delivered through statewide collaboration with early education partners.
• Student-Centered enrollment tracking and class scheduling.
o Delivered through consistent monitoring and student survey.
• Local collaboration will allow College of the Siskiyous to have an onsite Practicum site at Discovery
Child Development Center once the program is reaccredited.
o Delivered through the establishment of an ongoing COS MOU.
• Program Coordinator serves on California early Childhood Mentor Teacher team, working to provide
practicum sites throughout the county.
o Delivered through site evaluations and Mentor/Director Program assessment.
• Program Director offers support and referral to students in Child Development Permit Application,
degree completion, transfer information and referral to support as needed through
o Assessment of CHILD Development Permit applications.
o Collaboration with community partners in accessing support available through
o CDE Child Development Division
o QRIS- Quality Rating Improvement System
o First Five Siskiyou
• Program Director represents College of the Siskiyous on the Siskiyou (formerly Siskiyou-Shasta) Quality
Rating Improvement System team.
 Delivered through collaboration with local partners to implement the QRIS finding through
which our county receives state and federal quality improvement funding for early education
programs.
• Schedule and organize Advisory Committee Meetings.
• The Program Coordinator prepares and submits College of the Siskiyous ECE Program Annual Report to
Siskiyou early education network partners.
f) Curriculum is up-to-date and needs based.
In 2023 Academic Year ECE@COS updated and reviewed all classes through the Curriculum Committee.
Through this process we responded to program needs and requirements identified in the Program Update,
College of the Siskiyous’ Board Policies and Administrative Procedures, our CTE Advisory Committee feedback,
Student surveys and faculty input. Our updates reflect Outcomes Assessment as mandated through the SLO
Assessment Committee.
As part of this process we:

title: ECE Comprehensive Program Review   2023-2024 (Part 6)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Comprehensive Program Review 2023-2024
Submitted: 12/16/2024
• Early Childhood Education and Child Development (ECE)
• Patrice Thatcher-Stephens, Program Coordinator
• Aligned our Student Learning Outcomes with C-ID standards for our CID classes-those COS Child
Development, Family, and Education courses which articulate to other California Community
Colleges, California State Universities, the University of California system and (at COS) Southern
Oregon University.
• Added Learning Objectives, not typically included in College of the Siskiyous’ COR’s- Course
Outlines of Record-to reflect C-ID standards.
• Revised some Student Learning Outcomes to reflect Best Practice and assure that SLO’s are
measurable and concise.
• Updated all Distance Learning Addendums for our DL classes.
• Sent all course catalogue data through technical review.
• We adjusted our ECE 2502 Practicum class units to 1-unit Lecture with 3 units of lab to
facilitate articulation to Southern Oregon University and application to Commission on Teacher
Credentialing Child Development Permit requirements.
PIVOT STUDENT SUCCESS RATE and FTES
ECE Program at COS became early adopters of online LMS’s, first ETUDES and now Canvas and “hybrid”
Canvas/Zoom classes. Since Spring 2020 our classes have been available entirely online, sometimes
augmented by synchronous Zoom meetings. As in Distance Learning in general, we found that our new
cohorts of students represent a different demographic than prior Face-to-Face/Videoconference cohorts. The
COVID 19 Emergency triggered changes in both Modes of Instruction and Emergency Withdrawal protocol.
FTES decrease significantly as students who were adversely affected by the pandemic left classes.
Any decrease in FTES/Retention/Success Rates related to the COVID Emergency are both broadly
anomalous and individually understandable. Graduation rates remained stable, increasing slightly through
Spring 2020. This reflected steady program growth over the preceding 10 years. The effects of disruption to
College of the Siskiyous’ and ECE@COS’ processes and strategy are reflected in substantial decrease is
graduates in spring 2021 and 2022. We graduated students who persevered in their course of study to
complete their degrees. However, these numbers also reflect the interruption of our established course of
study by the disruption of face-to-face instruction. In spring 2023 we rebounded, wrangling students whose
progress had been interrupted and adding a few who were able to take advantage of our wider geographic
area. Seem PIVOT data above.
ECE@COS, and, in fact, COS in general, continues to be challenged in designing programs that both
build upon the advantages of our expanded and positively evolving Distance Learning opportunities and
maintaining the core values of personal connection and accessibility. Current initiatives to reimagine our Lab
Year Duplicated Head Count ECE Graduates

title: ECE Comprehensive Program Review   2023-2024 (Part 7)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Comprehensive Program Review 2023-2024
Submitted: 12/16/2024
• Early Childhood Education and Child Development (ECE)
• Patrice Thatcher-Stephens, Program Coordinator
School into a Distance Learning environment, increase face-to-face/synchronous Zoom and Hybrid Methods of
Instruction reflect our commitment to constant improvement and appropriate response to current higher
education reality.
Various program at College of the Siskiyous are discovering what Methods of Instruction best serve
their needs. For example, CTE programs often find that face-to-face instruction is necessary, effective, and
appropriate for the students they serve. Some General Education programs are primarily online, while others
best serve their students face-to-face. ECE@COS had historically used face-to-face/videoconference to serve
our students in Siskiyou and Modoc Counties. Subsequent to the accommodations described above we found
that we needed to make data based decisions in regard to Methods of Instruction. In spring 2023 we surveyed
our population to get an accurate snapshot of our current geographic distribution of students.

title: ECE Comprehensive Program Review   2023-2024 (Part 8)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Comprehensive Program Review 2023-2024
Submitted: 12/16/2024
• Early Childhood Education and Child Development (ECE)
• Patrice Thatcher-Stephens, Program Coordinator
This data illustrates the locations served by ECE@COS. Without our historic ability to videoconference
to distant sites, the most effective way to meet the needs of the most students is Online-Zoom Methods of
Instruction. Please see this link to view the departmental discussion.
ECE@COS Planning Power Point
g) Provide a breakdown of the classes offered in your areas within the Program Review cycle.
The Program Review Cycle at College of the Siskiyous has become significantly better monitored and
aligned over the past two years. As always, ECE, as a Career and Technical Education program, has completed a
program review every two years. The required format has become more consistent as we use an Annual Update to
reflect on the progress toward two-year goals; review our SLO assessment rotation and MOI’s. It is a joy to have a
competent and engaged team responsible for providing the needed data and administrative support for these
reviews. It is a Year One:
ECE Classes by Semester Classes required for degrees are in BLUE
Please refer to the “Guide to ECE@COS” posted on our Program Homepage https://www.siskiyous.edu/cte/ece/
Fall Classes
ECE 1001 Introduction to ECE Principles, Practices and Standards: This is the introductory class. It is a CORE class required
for the A.S./A.S.-T/Permits/Title 22 requirements. It is offered every fall.
ECE 1002 Child Development: This is a CORE class required for the A.S./A.S.-T/Permits/Title 22 requirements. This class also
satisfies the G.E. Social Sciences requirement. It is offered every fall.
ECE 1003 Child, Family and Community: This is a CORE class required for the A.S./A.S.-T/Child Development Permits/Title 22
requirements. This class also satisfies the G.E. Social Sciences requirement. It is offered every fall.
ECE 2000 Observing and Assessing--Desired Results and Developmentally Appropriate Environments: This class is required
for the A.S./A.S.-T/Advanced Permits. It is an advanced class—students should complete, or be enrolled in, the majority of their
CORE classes before enrolling in this class. It is offered every fall.
ECE 2002 Child Nutrition Health and Safety: This class is required for the A.S./A.S.-T/Advanced Permits. It is an advanced
class—students should complete, or be enrolled in, the majority of their CORE classes before enrolling in this class. It is offered every
fall.
ECE 1999 Human Development: This class is NOT required for the A.S./A.S.-T/Child Development Permits. However, it does
satisfy a CORE class requirement (can replace ECE 1002) for the Child Development Permit. This class also satisfies the G.E. Social
Sciences requirement. It is offered every fall and spring.
ECE 2003 Serving Children and Families with Special Needs: This class is NOT required for the A.S./A.S.-T/satisfies Master
Teacher Permit specialization. It is offered in the spring.
ECE 2527 Supervision of Adult Staff in Early Education: This class is NOT required for the A.S./A.S.-T/required for Site
Supervisor or Program Director Permits/required for Mentor Teachers. It is offered in the fall.
Spring Classes
ECE 1500 Introduction to Curriculum: This is a CORE class required for the A.S./A.S.-T/Child Development Permits/Title 22
requirements. It is offered every spring.
ECE 1501 Positive Child Guidance: This class is NOT required for the A.S./A.S.-T/ applies to Master Teacher Permit
Specializations. It is offered in the spring.
ECE 1502 Serving Children and Families in a Diverse Society: This is a class required for the A.S./A.S.-T/ applies to Master
Teacher Permit Specializations. This class also satisfies the G.E. Diversity requirement. Check the Course Schedule for availability
I tis offered every year either in fall or spring.
ECE 1999 Human Development: See Above
ECE 2500 Family Relationships and Parenting: This class is NOT required for the A.S./A.S.-T/ applies to Master Teacher
Permit Specializations. However, it does satisfy a CORE class requirement (can replace ECE 1003) for the Child Development
Permit. This class also satisfies the G.E. Diversity requirement. It is offered in the spring.
ECE 2501 Administration: This class is NOT required for the A.S./A.S.-T/ required for Site Supervisor or Program Director
Permits/required for Mentor Teachers. It is offered in the spring.

title: ECE Comprehensive Program Review   2023-2024 (Part 10)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Comprehensive Program Review 2023-2024
Submitted: 12/16/2024
• Early Childhood Education and Child Development (ECE)
• Patrice Thatcher-Stephens, Program Coordinator
ECE 2502 Practicum: This is a class required for the A.S./A.S.-T/ Child Development Permits. This is a lecture/Lab class
requiring 6 per week hours of work in an approved early education program. The California State Department of Education and
Community Care Licensing Department of Social Services requires that students satisfy immunization requirements. See most
current Update on following pages. It is offered in the spring.
ECE 2510 Creative Art Activities: This class is NOT required for the A.S./A.S.-T/ applies to Master Teacher Permit
Specializations. It is offered in the spring.
2. External Factors with Significant Impact: What external factors have a significant impact
on your program?
a) Budgetary constraints and opportunities.
During the 2023-24 academic year, ECE@COS benefitted from significant budgetary support based on
our ability to adapt and retain enrollment. Perkins funding supported a transformation of the Lab School into a
potential Zoom teaching space.
b) Competition from other institutions.
We prefer to collaborate rather than compete. The majority of ECE programs and degrees at CCC’s
throughout California are aligned. Many have classes available online. Of course, we welcome transfer
students from other CCC’s. We offer our own aligned classes online and meet students enrolled at our cohort
colleges online. When helpful and appropriate we refer our students, who need classes.
On the other hand, the promulgation of for-profit online institutions, while not specifically affecting
our core program, increasingly affects the quality of the course of study available to educators, and early
educators. Online education is great. Accessibility is important. Quality is fundamental.
Then, there are the California State Universities. The California State Department of Education’s
Transitional Kindergarten initiative has affected CCC ECE programs as primary and elementary teachers return
to community colleges to qualify for work with younger children in these classrooms. We initially created
pathways specifically designed for credentialed primary teachers and welcome them into our Early Childhood
Education and Child Development classes. Year’s of work and CCC’s excitement at being included in teacher
preparation was met by a turf war initiated by CSU’s. This is discouraging and disappointing. We are virtually
excluded from work specific to preparing teacher for TK or for the always elusive P3 Credential.
Related to this, somehow…is the school district’s cannibalizing of the California State Preschool
structure to move under-age children to TK, out of CSPS without appropriate attention to Developmental
Theory. To be continued.
c) Requirements of four-year institutions
Because our program is aligned through CAP and is an AD-T program with an articulation agreement
with Southern Oregon University, we benefit for the oversight and rigorous academic requirements included
in those agreements.
d) Institutional regulation, policies, standards, and other mandates

title: ECE Comprehensive Program Review   2023-2024 (Part 12)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Comprehensive Program Review 2023-2024
Submitted: 12/16/2024
• Early Childhood Education and Child Development (ECE)
• Patrice Thatcher-Stephens, Program Coordinator
The Early Childhood Education and Child Development Program at College of the Siskiyous prepares
students for Title 5 Child Development Permits issued through the California State Department of Education
Commission on Teacher Credentialing. Beyond the course work required for our local A.A. and AD-T degrees,
we offer classes fulfilling the CTC requirements. Our program offers classes meeting the Title 22 Department
of Social Services Community Care Licensing requirements for teachers employed in independent early
education programs.
In addition to offering these classes, we direct students to appropriate ‘permit’ classes, scaffold permit
application and refer students to sources of funding as appropriate. See following page.
Child Development Permit Matrix –College of the Siskiyous Classes Meeting Education Requirements for Matrix Option 1
Assistant Teacher 6 units of any ECE COS classes (2 3-unit classes)
Associate Teacher Required: 1002 Child Development OR 1999 Human Development/1003 Child, Family and Community OR 2500 Family
Relationships and Parenting/1500 Introduction to Curriculum. Plus: One more 3-unit class
Teacher Required: 1002 Child Development OR 1999 Human Development/1003 Child, Family and Community OR 2500 Family
Relationships and Parenting/1500 Introduction to Curriculum Plus: Five more 3-unit classes Plus: 16 units of degree
applicable General Education Units (in any of the following: English/Math OR Science/Social Sciences/Humanities OR Fine
Arts)
Master Teacher Required: 1002 Child Development OR 1999 Human Development/1003 Child, Family and Community OR 2500 Family
Relationships and Parenting/1500 Introduction to Curriculum Plus: Five more 3-unit classes Plus: 16 units of degree
applicable General Education Units (in any of the following: English/Math OR Science/Social Sciences/Humanities OR Fine
Arts) Plus: 6 units of ECE Specialization (Classes can be ‘double counted’). See Master Teacher Specializations on following
pages. Plus: 2527=2 units of Adult Supervision
Site Supervisor Required: AA (or 60 units) in ECE including: Required: 1002 Child Development OR 1999 Human Development/1003 Child,
Family and Community OR 2500 Family Relationships and Parenting/1500 Introduction to Curriculum Plus: Five more 3-unit
classes Plus: 2501 Administration AND 2525 Staffing=6 administrative units Plus: 2527=2 units of Adult Supervision
e) Non-Institutional regulations, policies, standards, and other mandates
Within both California State Title 5 and Title 22 requirements DOJ and California State Penal Code
requirements may affect our students’ application of Child Development Permit application or employment in
a DSS CCL center or program. Students participating in our clinical Practicum classes must comply with
California State SB792 requiring immunizations of all employees and volunteers in a childcare setting who
provide “care and supervision” of children. These immunizations include influenza, pertussis, and measles.
Nationally, Head Start Standards inform our course content and SLO’s. These standards apply to
students whose employment goals include Head Start. Since Siskiyou County is the home of three separate
agencies with Head Start grants, Head Start standards significantly affect education and employment in our
area.
The National Associate for the Education of Young Children is the professional organization setting the
standard to Best Practice in early education. The CAP alignment project reflects NAEYC standards and guides
CCC ECE programs. Additionally, the NAEYC through the National Academy of Early Education is the nation al
accrediting body for early education programs. Discovery Child Development Center is the only NAEYC
Accredited early education program in Siskiyou County. Based on this accreditation our students are given an
opportunity to receive clinical practicum practice in a center where Best Practice is a standard.
The CDE’s Transitional Kindergarten initiative has affected CCC ECE programs as primary and
elementary teachers return to community colleges to qualify for work with younger children in these
classrooms. CCC’s designed a system to accommodate these students. See 2b above.
f) Job Market
• Employer Requirements

title: ECE Comprehensive Program Review   2023-2024 (Part 14)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Comprehensive Program Review 2023-2024
Submitted: 12/16/2024
• Early Childhood Education and Child Development (ECE)
• Patrice Thatcher-Stephens, Program Coordinator
Requirements of prospective employers reflect the legal and educational requirement outlined
above. Locally, our successful students are almost fully employed. Many students obtain entry-level
jobs before starting ECE classes. This reflects our alignment with both Title 5 and Title 22 standards.
Additionally, prospective employers value a familiarity with our rural community and the growing
diversity of Siskiyou County children.
Unfortunately, with the exception of Head Start and California State Preschool programs,
Siskiyou County employers often underpay and provide no benefits to new graduates. Fortunately,
most COS graduates gain experience and/or their B.A. degrees and move into higher paid Head Start or
State Preschool jobs.
• Developments in the Field
In regard to the Job Market, development in the field (both current and future) will continue to
reflect the increasingly rigorous standards in early education. It is anticipated that, in California, a very long
anticipated renovation of Title 5 and Title 22 standards will reflect the influence of the Transitional
Kindergarten Initiative, the P3Credential, and community standards. If this change occurs, CCC ECE classes
and community early education programs will adapt, once again, to changing standards,
ECE@COS will continue to work within the existing system. We work with our cohorts throughout
the state to advocate for children and families and to educate early educators to support those families
using research based Best Practice and philosophies of education emphasizing tested and implemented
methods.
3. Progress on Outcomes Assessment:
The ECE program has traditionally maintained a schedule of Program Learning Outcomes and Student
Learning Outcomes Assessment. Please see response to this section, including improvements made as a result
of our ongoing assessment. See 1(f) above re course updates. We are committed to aligning our Course SLO’s
with Program SLO’s. This initiative was advanced through the work of the College of the Siskiyous Academic
Senate SLO Committee over the past 2 years.
4. Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators
First, what is working. The CTE Program seems to be functioning well. The recent administrative
turnover in CTE program administration has been pretty seamless, in our program’s experience. Our
administrators are skilled and accessible. Processes—e.g. Program Review are defined and monitored. Since
Career and Technical Education is the immediate context in which ECE exists, we remain very grateful.
However, the college continues to need to clearly define itself and commit to a reasonable size, methods of
instruction and administrative structure. Our Human Resource staff still appears challenged in meeting HR
needs. Our IT department staff are individually very skilled and helpful. wonderful. However, it seems
impossible to accomplish any technical renovation or assessment. Our Maintenance people are, apparently,
overworked, but we still need to be able to depend upon them. The Office of Instruction seems to have made
impressive progress in hiring and cross training. Like I said a year ago, we’re tired. We are going to continue to
be tired if we don’t hire the staff to do the work that needs to be done.
Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators:
Please discuss your program’s performance on each program specific data item provided by
the Research Office.

title: ECE Comprehensive Program Review   2023-2024 (Part 16)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Comprehensive Program Review 2023-2024
Submitted: 12/16/2024
• Early Childhood Education and Child Development (ECE)
• Patrice Thatcher-Stephens, Program Coordinator
PIVOT Retention Please see section 1.f
PIVOT Success Please see section 1.f
Student CRN Data: This data is super interesting, I do not see any outstanding or surprising trends. Online
students are over all less successful by percentage, and summer students on line appear to be more successful
by percentage. I would hypothesize that this is a combination of online detachment, summer-session student
commitment, and time to focus on individual classes. There is no disparity between fully time faculty and
adjunct faculty.
PIVOT Race Data Please see discussion in section 1.c
PIVOT Gender Date Please seem the discussion in section 1.c
FTES Please see discussion 1.f
PIVOT Assignment Type is it has been since the beginning…the ECE Program is 40/60 Fulltime/Adjunct.
We do fine. I spent my first ten years at College of the Siskiyous arguing for another full- time instructor. Once
we actually got on the hiring list. However, I understand that this is not a priority. We are a relatively low FTES
program and it is a monetary decision.
As the argument goes, full time instructors—in any area- are invested, connected and supportive in
ways that those with other ‘day jobs’ just cannot be. Our students, the community and the program would
benefit from that. On the other hand, our adjuncts are well qualified, connected to the community and hard
working.
5. Other Unit-Specific Quantitative and Qualitative Research: Please provide
a) List of any quantitative or qualitative measures not provided in the previous questions that you have chosen
to gauge your program’s effectiveness (e.g., transfers, degrees, certificates, satisfaction, student contacts,
student headcount, Perkin’s data, equity data, etc.)
Child Development Permits California offers six levels of Child Development Permits, each with its own
set of issuance requirements and each authorizing the holder to perform different levels of service in child
development programs. The State Permits are issued by the California Department of Education, the same
agency that issues Teaching Credentials for Public School Teaching. Permits are required if you work for a
school that is funded by the Department of Education or Head Start.
The ECE Program at College of the Siskiyous does not award “Certificates” rather we encourage and
scaffold students’ application for a CCT Child Development Permit. These permits, unlike certificates, are
recognized in early education and qualify students to work in early education programs. Each year 7-10
current or former COS students apply and receive Child Development permits.
Perkins Data is collected through the federal government in support of the Carl D. Perkins Career and
Technical Education Act of 2007. Data is sourced from community colleges, trade schools and other state and
county agencies. The CTE Perkins Coordinator has contacted CTE programs, including ECE. We appreciate this
interest. The assembled data was shared with me, as Program Coordinator. As a result, I understand that our
program is failing to meet Non-Traditional student equity goals. This is understandable. ECE is a ‘woman
heavy’ profession for historic and economic reasons.
North Far North Regional Labor Market Assessment reflects regional trends, but does not sufficiently
recognize the unique aspects of Siskiyou. Please refer to "Regional Labor Market Assessment" from the CTE
North Far North Region Center on Excellence at
http://www.siskiyous.edu/cte/ece/ for current local data.
NAEYC Accreditation process continues to be challenging. This is the gold standard for assessing early
education environments. We struggle to meet these standards. This is primarily the result of the Child

title: ECE Comprehensive Program Review   2023-2024 (Part 17)
text:
-- 11 of 14 --

College of the Siskiyous Comprehensive Program Review 2023-2024
Submitted: 12/16/2024
• Early Childhood Education and Child Development (ECE)
• Patrice Thatcher-Stephens, Program Coordinator
Development and Early Education’s Program Director’s badly informed decision in entering into an MOU with
an unprepared agency. The program is currently on Step 1 Enrollment and gathering documentation required
to complete Step 2 Application. See https://www.naeyc.org/accreditation
b) A summary of the results of these measures indicates that Child Development Permits are important and
valuable indicators of student success. Perkins Data appears to accurately reflect current workplace
conditions. Additionally, accurate data that reflects employment is necessary. NFN Regional Center is a fairly
accurate source for employment data for Siskiyous County. The NAEYC Accreditation Site is the source for
guiding programs through the complex accreditation process and is not easily navigable. They do offer
individual program assistance. We have A January meeting scheduled.
c) Please see comments included in the context of 5a).
6. Evaluation: Based upon and not repeating the descriptions you provided in Question 1
and the responses provided in Questions 2 – 5, please provide an analysis of what is going well and why
and what is not going well and why, in any of the following areas that are relevant to your program:
a) Representativeness of population served
In regard to Ethnicity and Race, as a result of the ever-increasing number of students we are recruiting
from throughout California, our population is very representative and appropriate for those working desiring
to work in early education in California. This is significantly important in supporting College of the Siskiyous’
and the California Community College System’s commitment to Equity and Social Justice.
Regarding gender, we are representative of current practice have come to understand that this
inequity is systemic and not unique.
b) Alternative modes and schedules of delivery (e.g., online, hybrid, early morning, evening services)
ECE had a long history of getting to students where they are when they need classes. Please see prior
discussion. We are great.
c) Partnerships (internal and external)
Externally ECE@COS is a recognized model of collaboration. In a huge county, with few people,
agencies learn to work together
d) Innovation and implementation of best practices
In early education, Best Practice is embedded in everything that we do. The educational philosophies
that we teach in our classes guide our work as educators. Assessment, Observation and Evaluation are as
central to each class session as they are to our students’ work with children and families.
e) Efficiency in operations
We are efficient. See our budget and class scheduling.
f) Efficiency in resource use
See above.
g) Staffing
Please see 4. PIVOT Assignment Type
h) Participation in shared governance (e.g., do unit members feel they participate effectively in planning and
decision-making?)

-- 12 of 14 --

title: ECE Comprehensive Program Review   2023-2024 (Part 18)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Comprehensive Program Review 2023-2024
Submitted: 12/16/2024
• Early Childhood Education and Child Development (ECE)
• Patrice Thatcher-Stephens, Program Coordinator
The Program Coordinator has a long history of active and involved participation in shared governance.
Adjunct Instructor and Program Coordinator interact often and meet each semester to plan and assess.
i) Professional development and training
Through professional organizations, NAEYC, CDTC, PITC, ESTEM, QRIS, Head Start, CCCECE and others
the Program Coordinator and Adjunct Instructors remain current and active in local, state professional
development.
j) Compliance with applicable mandates
The ECE Program is currently in compliance with internal and external program mandates.
7. Vision. Tell us your unit’s vision: Where would you like your program to be four years
from now? Dream big while considering any upcoming changes (e.g., new buildings, labs,
growth, changes in the discipline, etc.)
We visualize that the Child Development, Family and Education Program will maintain its role as an
aligned and committed member of the CCC ECE community. We will continue to serve our students, and the
children and families in Siskiyou County and to collaborate with the myriad of agencies that are part of our
network.
We aligned and mapped our courses to allow us to serve students within the context of workforce
development which is heavily invested in alignment and mapping. Our core course will remain constant.
However, as the field grows and expands to meet greater demand, both for services and for skills, we will add
additions course of study options to meet, those needs.
Currently, our most significant goal for ECE@COS is supporting the Siskiyou Child Care Council-
Discovery Child Development Center’s NAEYC Accreditation. That process encapsulates the creation of a
functioning Lab School and effective Distance Learning hub. Success is not guaranteed. If we do not achieve
accreditation, the MOU should not be renewed.
8. Progress on Prior Goals: Briefly summarize any progress your unit has made in meeting
the goals and objectives identified in the program’s last Four-Year Action Plan.
My responses regarding previous Comprehensive Program Review Goals are below in blue.
• OEI Training and Program Preparation per above with an extended timeline. The long-term goal is to
translate ECE@COS into an online degree program while maintaining Best Practice, enrollment, and
locally relevant community involvement. This includes FTF/VC classes as appropriate.
Our DL structure is strong, and we do not intend to pursue OEI alignment. Please see our survey of
student locations and our conclusions.
• Align our Core Courses to the ECE Competencies Mapping Tool per above. This initiative is vital to
meeting ECE Guided Pathways goals and is fully funded and pending per gridlocked contract
negotiations.
DONE
• Meet ECE@COS’ MOU obligation to Maintain Discovery Child Development Center NAEYC
Accreditation.
This is stalled. Our collaboration with Siskiyou Child Care Council does not need these standards.
• Maintain materials and equipment necessary to demonstrate Best Practices in Early Education in
Lecture and Lab classes

10. Closing the Loop
In the last budget cycle, I believe that our CQUIPS we not approved. We did complete, somewhat successfully,
approvals from previous years. During the 2023-2024 Academic Year the Finance Office was in transition, and
later in the cycle, the Career and Technical Education’s budget process was in affected by Perkins and Strong
Workforce funding changes.
We certify that the ECE@COS Program Review process included broad dialogue about student
learning, program effectiveness, and resource allocation.
Patrice Thatcher-Stephens
Preparer
